skill.name: vector-index-tuning
skill.description: Optimize vector index performance for latency, recall, and memory. Use when tuning HNSW parameters, selecting quantization strategies, or scaling vector search infrastructure.
skill.summary: Tune vector search indexes with benchmark templates, HNSW guidance, quantization options, and monitoring patterns.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found command, network, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns, but review shows these are documentation false positives. The skill is a Markdown guide with Python examples for vector index tuning and no evidence of prompt injection, exfiltration, or malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misidentified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The detected backticks are Markdown code fence delimiters around tables and Python examples, not Ruby or shell backtick execution. No command execution instruction or runtime script was found at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are visible Markdown fence markers around static documentation. There is no executable Ruby or shell syntax at those lines.

low_findings.title: Documentation Links Misidentified as Runtime Network Access
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The hardcoded URLs are resource links to the HNSW paper, Faiss wiki, and ANN benchmarks. They are not invoked by code and do not send data to external services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are Markdown references under a Resources heading. No fetch, request, credential handling, or telemetry behavior is present.

low_findings.title: Qdrant Memory Mapping Configuration Misidentified as File Access
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged memmap_threshold values configure when Qdrant may use disk-backed storage. They do not read arbitrary files, write user data, or access local paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are Qdrant optimizer configuration values inside a sample collection setup. No filesystem path or direct file operation appears in the reviewed context.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Pattern Has No Cryptographic Context
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged lines contain the skill description and vector quantization variables such as codes and codebooks. No hashing, encryption, signing, or weak cryptographic algorithm usage was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the flagged lines found vector compression terminology, not cryptographic operations. No evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or similar weak algorithms was found.

content.value_statement: Vector search systems are hard to tune because latency, recall, memory, and build time conflict. This skill provides practical templates for HNSW tuning, quantization, Qdrant configuration, and monitoring.
actual_capabilities: Recommends index types for small, medium, large, and billion-scale vector collections.
Provides Python templates for benchmarking HNSW parameters against recall and latency targets.
Shows scalar, product, and binary quantization examples for memory reduction.
Includes Qdrant collection settings for recall, speed, balanced, and memory-focused goals.
Provides monitoring templates for latency percentiles, recall, QPS, and build throughput.
limitations: Requires users to supply their own vectors, queries, and ground truth data.
Does not run benchmarks automatically or validate results against live infrastructure.
Example code assumes Python libraries such as NumPy, hnswlib, scikit-learn, and Qdrant client.
Recommendations are starting points and need production testing before deployment.

use_cases.title: Tune Search Recall
use_cases.description: Compare HNSW M, efConstruction, and efSearch settings against a recall target and latency budget.

use_cases.title: Reduce Vector Memory Use
use_cases.description: Estimate storage needs and choose quantization strategies for high-volume embedding collections.

use_cases.title: Configure Qdrant Collections
use_cases.description: Create Qdrant collection settings aligned to recall, speed, balanced, or memory priorities.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Starting Index
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning a new vector store.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use vector-index-tuning to recommend an index type for 800,000 vectors with 768 dimensions. Prioritize balanced recall and latency.

prompt_templates.title: Estimate Memory Needs
prompt_templates.prompt: Use vector-index-tuning to estimate memory for 25 million 1536-dimensional vectors using HNSW with fp32, int8, and product quantization.

prompt_templates.title: Plan an HNSW Benchmark
prompt_templates.prompt: Use vector-index-tuning to design an HNSW benchmark for target recall 0.97, p95 latency under 20 ms, and 5 million vectors.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize a Qdrant Deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Use vector-index-tuning to propose Qdrant HNSW, quantization, optimizer, and search parameter settings for a memory-constrained semantic search service.

output_examples.input: Recommend a vector index for 2 million vectors with a 95 percent recall target.
output_examples.output: Use HNSW with quantization as the starting point. Begin with moderate graph connectivity, benchmark search breadth, and compare recall before reducing memory further.

output_examples.input: How should I reduce memory for a large embedding collection?
output: Use product quantization when memory pressure is high.
Measure recall loss with real queries before deploying.

output_examples.input: What should I monitor after changing index settings?
output_examples.output: Track p50, p95, and p99 latency, recall against ground truth, query throughput, build time, and memory use after each configuration change.

best_practices: Benchmark with production-like queries and ground truth sets before changing defaults.
Tune one variable at a time so recall, latency, and memory tradeoffs stay clear.
Monitor recall and latency continuously after index rebuilds or data distribution changes.
anti_patterns: Do not choose aggressive quantization without measuring recall loss.
Do not optimize only average latency when p95 and p99 latency affect users.
Do not ignore build time and reindexing cost for frequently updated collections.

faq.question: Does this skill build vector indexes automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and Python templates that users adapt to their own systems.

faq.question: Which vector databases does it mention directly?
faq.answer: It includes Qdrant configuration examples and general guidance that can inform other vector search systems.

faq.question: Can it help with billion-scale vector search?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes when to consider IVF, product quantization, DiskANN, and tiered storage patterns.

faq.question: Does it require ground truth data?
faq.answer: Ground truth data is needed to measure recall accurately. Without it, latency results are incomplete.

faq.question: Is quantization always recommended?
faq.answer: No. Quantization saves memory, but users should test recall and consider full precision for maximum accuracy.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The detected security patterns were reviewed as documentation false positives, with no confirmed malicious behavior.
